Lato Offices of
OLSON, CONNELLY HAGEL,
July 31-, L99O
FONG & LEIDIGH
IIAND DEIJIVERED
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 rrJrr Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 9581,4
REs YOUR I,ETTER REGARDTNG LETTER NO. 90-492
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
I am in receipt of your letter dated July 30,
l-99o, nrith regard to my recent letter to you (dated
July 23). Although your letter appears to be a form
letter in part, since it makes specific reference to my
conversation with John Wallace on July 26, I want to
make sure the record is clear.
I provided you with a series of ttreal life hypo-
thetical guestionsr' for consideration by the staff in
its rnemorandum to the Cornmissioners regarding advice
the staff is giving on certain questions discussed at
Iast monthrs Commission meeting. It was my understand-
ing from the discussion at the trpre-agendatt meeting on
JuIy 23 that such guestions were appropriate for sub-
mission to the staff for discussion at the next Commis-
sion meeting.
on JuIy 26, John l{a11ace phoned me to say that the
staff did not intend to address my guestions in the
memorandum for the upcorning Cournission meeting. The
staff intended to focus the memorandum in another area.
Instead the staff wanted to treat ny letter as one for
rrinformal assistance.rr Mr. Wallace was seeking rny
concurrence in that transfornation. I explained to Mr.
Wallace that I was reluctant to agree, since an rrinfor-
mal assistancerr response night be months in arriving.
The guestions posed in my letter are ones which must be
dealt with before the November election cycle begins.
Mr. I{allace gave me his personal assurance that my
letter would be responded to within 2L working days, ds
if it were a rrformal advicerr letter. 
He pointed out
that it would have an rrlrr number rather than an rrArr
number. With that assurance, I agreed to my letter
being treated as one seeking rrinformal assistance.
Therefore, it would not need to be addressed in,

consequently, it is my express understanding that my July

John Wallace

LERoY Y FoNG

OF CouNsEL

300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, Califomia 95814
TELEPHoNE: (916) 442-2952
F^x, 1916) 442-7280
Lau offic* of
OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDICH
JuIy 23, ]-990
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CO}.{MISSION
428 rtJtt Street, suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814
IIAND DEI,IVERED
RE: QUESTIONS FOR sTAEtrI8 CONSIDEFETTON REGARDING
rpERgoNAL, USEI AND rTRUSTrr PROVISIONS OP TIIE ACT
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
At the last commission meeting, interested persons
were invited to provide hypothetical questions to the
staff. 
These would be considered by staff in preparing
a memorandum for the Commissionrs next neeting. The
purpose of the nemorandum is to review staff ansvrers to
advj-ce questions on the subject of the rrpersonal usert
and trtrust[ provisions of the Act.
I submit the following rrreal 1ife" hypothetical
questions for the staffts consideration and responses.
These questions relate to items (1), (3) and (6) on
pages 8-9 of the last staff nenorandum.
(1) Ba11ot measure committees: staffrs
memorandum advises that a candidate nay contribute to a
balfot measure committee ttif the measure concerns an
issue which the candidate has made a part of his or her
campaign. rl
(a) A local elected official 
has three more
years to run in her current term. There is a statewide
ballot neasure on the issue of abortion. The local
official 
wishes to contribute from campaign funds to
the ba1lot measure in order to demonstrate her support
for the issue. She knows nany of her constituents feel
the abortion issue is very important. However, she had
no opposition in her last re-election canpaign and
1ike1y will have no opposition in her next campaign.
consequently, it is impossible to te11 at this time
whether the abortion question will be an issue in her
next campaign three years hence. May she contribute
with campaign funds?
(b) A local- elected official 
has three more
years to run on his current term. Rent control is an
irnportant issue in his community. In another 1ocal


$$. !1'\ sz 1'
t?,11[
',1 ':l d J


Scott Hallabrin
July 23, 1990
Page 2
jurisdiction 
(100 rniles away) there is a ballot measure dealing
with rent control . He wishes to contribute to the balIot measure
in order to show his support for the issue. There is a case
currently pending before the United States Supreme Court which
may resolve all rent control issues on constitutional grounds.
The case will be decided after the ba11ot measure is voted on,
but before the local elected official rs next canpaign. Depending
on the result of the supreme court decision, the rent control
issue may be moot long before the local elected officialrs 
next
re-election campaign. May he contribute to the ballot measure
with his campaign funds?
(c) A local elected official believes strongly that
smoking and its side effects (including trsecond-handrr srnoke) are
detrirnental to humanst health. she has been in the forefront of
efforts at the local level to adopt and inrplernent no snoking
ordinances governing public buildings of her local jurisdiction.
She is in the first year of a four-year tern. 
Tobacco industry
lobbyists are actively lobbying the state legislature to adopt
state-wide standards for regulating srnoking in public (and
private buildings). 
The anti-smoking forces are considering
circulating a statewide initiative 
to counter such legislation.
They have solicited her for a contributj,on. In her recent
election campaign, growth-controI, gang violence and a sports
stadium were the major campaign issues. The snoking ordinance
was not. However, there is strong public support in her
community for the ordinance. May she contribute to the statewide
ba11ot measure campaign fron her campaign funds?
(d) A state legislator represents a district 
which
includes a jurisdiction with a strong rent control ordinance.
His next election uiIl be in two years. Another jurisdiction
rrhich has a sinilar strong rent control ordinance has a ballot
measure to be voted on this year which would significantly 
weaken
that jurisdictionrs rent control ordinance. 
Tenant activists in
the legislator's district 
fear that if the balIot measure
succeeds, it will increase the likelihood of a sinil,ar measure
being proposed in their jurisdiction. 
They urge the legislator
to contribute to the carnpaj,gn against the ballot measure. lilay he
do so with his campaign funds?
(e) suppose instead of a baLlot measure, there is a
Iawsuit which has been brought challenging the constitutionality
of the rent control measure. The defenders of the other
jurisdictionrs rent control measure are raising funds to provide
for the Iega1 defense of the measure, May the legislator


Scott Hallabrin
July 23, L99O
Page 3
contribute to the l-egal defense fund froro carnpaign funds? would
the answer be different in either of the above situations if the
ballot measure or lawsuit involved the rent control ordinance in
the legislatorrs own district?
(3) Use of carnpaign funds to contribute to a federal
California candidate. Staffrs nemorandum advises that generally,
such contributions are not perrnissible, absent a showinq that the
expenditure is associated with the candidaters office or
election.
(a) A local candidate wj-shes to attend the fundraiser
of a local candidate for federal office. 
The purpose of
attending is to demonstrate solidarity with the federal candidate
(and thereby acquire some party identification for a rrnon-
partisan'r office). 
uay the local candidate use campaign funds to
purchase a ticket to the fundraj-ser?
(b) A 1ocaI candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser
of a candidate for federal office from a different Iocale. 
The
purpose of attending is to mingle with contributors to the
federal candidate who rnight be induced to contribute to the local
candidate, since the te/o share similar views on many issues. May
the locat candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to
the fundraiser?
(c) A 1ocaI candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser
of a Ioca1 candidate for federal office. 
The purpose of
attending is to show that the locaI office candidate has the
ability to rrget the earrr of the candidate for federal office.
This would denonstrate the local candidate's ability to seek
federal assistance for issues of local concern. IIay the local
candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the
fundraiser?
(d) A legislative candidate wishes to attend a
fundraiser for her partyrs candidate for President. The
candidate is from Texas, not California. 
May she use campaign
funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?
(6) contributions to out-of-state political 
committees.
staffts memorandum advises the general rute j,s that a candidate
may not give campaign funds to out-of-state commj-ttees.
(a) A locaI candidate has two years to run on her
term. She may or may not decide to seek re-election; however,
she has filed a Forn 501 for re-election. 
she, and many of her
constituents, are cornmitted on the abortion issue. A ballot
measure is pending in Arizona on the abortion question. It is


Scott Eallabrin
JuIy 23, 1,99 0
Page 4
expected to make the test case which the united states supreme
court will use to deternine the continuing applicability of Roe
v. wade. May she use campaign funds to contribute to the ballot
measure commi.ttee in Arizona?
(b) A locat candidate is running for election in a
district 
whose voters are predoninantly Black. Andrew Young is
in a run-off for the nomination for Governor of Georgia. May the
local candidate use campaign funds to contribute to Andrew Young?
(c) suppose the candidate in question (b) above is a
candidate for state legislative office in a district 
whose voters
are predominantly Black. would the answer be any different?
(d) suppose the candidate in question (c) above is
runninq in a district with no significant Black population.
would the answer be any different?
(e) A candidate for local office is a wornan. She is
receiving campaign funding support fron the National Womenrs
Political" caucus. She has been asked to, in turn, supply
carnpaign funding support for other wornen candidates in other
states. 
May she use her campaign funds for this purpose?
As I understand it, the connission has asked the staff to
just provide Yes or No answers to the guestions, rather than 90
into great detail as to the reasoning. In that spirit, 
I have
not alked for the reasons why the answers night differ between
various guestions posed herein. r hope that these questions are
helpful to the staff in indicating its interpretation. 
I look
forward to reading the answers'
very truly yours,
---QQSON, CONNELLY,
